content:
# Exodus Chapter 3 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~32v–33r (PDF pp. 63–64); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 43–44 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_003_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_003_page_002.jpg`

---

**1** Now Moses was shepherding the flock of Jethro his father-in-law, the priest of Midian. And he led the flock beyond the wilderness and came to the mountain of God, to Horeb.

**2** And the angel of the LORD appeared to him in a flame of fire from the midst of a bush. And he looked, and behold, the bush was burning with fire, yet the bush was not consumed.

**3** And Moses said, "Let me turn aside now and see this great sight -- why the bush does not burn up."

**4** And the LORD saw that he turned aside to see, and God called to him from the midst of the bush and said, "Moses, Moses!" And he said, "Here I am."

**5** And He said, "Do not draw near to this place. Remove your sandals from your feet, for the place upon which you are standing is holy ground."

**6** And He said, "I am the God of your father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob." And Moses hid his face, for he was afraid to look upon God.

**7** And the LORD said, "I have surely seen the affliction of My people who are in Egypt, and I have heard their cry because of their taskmasters, for I know their sufferings.

**8** And I have come down to deliver them from the hand of Egypt, and to bring them up from that land to a good and spacious land, to a land flowing with milk and honey, to the place of the Canaanite and the Hittite and the Amorite and the Perizzite and the Hivite and the Jebusite.

**9** And now, behold, the cry of the sons of Israel has come to Me, and I have also seen the oppression with which the Egyptians oppress them.

**10** And now, go, and I will send you to Pharaoh, that you may bring My people, the sons of Israel, out of Egypt."

**11** And Moses said to God, "Who am I, that I should go to Pharaoh, and that I should bring the sons of Israel out of Egypt?"

**12** And He said, "For I will be with you, and this shall be the sign for you that I have sent you: when you bring the people out of Egypt, you shall serve God upon this mountain."

**13** And Moses said to God, "Behold, when I come to the sons of Israel and say to them, 'The God of your fathers has sent me to you,' and they say to me, 'What is His name?' -- what shall I say to them?"

**14** And God said to Moses, "I AM WHO I AM." And He said, "Thus you shall say to the sons of Israel: 'I AM has sent me to you.'"

**15** And God said further to Moses, "Thus you shall say to the sons of Israel: 'The LORD, the God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, has sent me to you.' This is My name forever, and this is My memorial from generation to generation.

**16** Go, and gather the elders of Israel, and say to them, 'The LORD, the God of your fathers, has appeared to me, the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, saying: I have surely attended to you and to what is being done to you in Egypt.

**17** And I have said, I will bring you up from the affliction of Egypt to the land of the Canaanite and the Hittite and the Amorite and the Perizzite and the Hivite and the Jebusite, to a land flowing with milk and honey.'

**18** And they will listen to your voice, and you shall come, you and the elders of Israel, to the king of Egypt, and you shall say to him, 'The LORD, the God of the Hebrews, has met with us. And now, let us go, we pray, a journey of three days into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God.'

**19** But I know that the king of Egypt will not give you leave to go, except by a mighty hand.

**20** And I will stretch out My hand and strike Egypt with all My wonders which I will do in its midst, and after that he will send you out.

**21** And I will give this people favor in the eyes of Egypt, and it shall be that when you go, you shall not go empty-handed.

**22** And each woman shall ask of her neighbor, and of the woman sojourning in her house, articles of silver and articles of gold, and garments, and you shall put them upon your sons and upon your daughters. And you shall plunder Egypt."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folio ~32v (PDF p. 63) begins with the closing verses of Exodus 2 and transitions into Exodus 3 in the second and third columns. The text of chapter 3 continues onto folio ~33r (PDF p. 64), where it completes through verse 22 and flows into the opening of chapter 4. The three-column layout is clearly legible, with Masorah parva notations in the margins and interlinear vowel pointing throughout. The Vaticanus LXX (pp. 43-44) was consulted as a secondary witness; the Greek text begins Exodus 3 on page 43 (marked CAP. XLVIII at top) and continues through page 44 (CAP. XLIX). The uncial script is continuous without word division, requiring careful parsing.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"Jethro" / "Reuel" (v. 1):** In Exodus 2:18, the same figure was named Reuel; here the MT reads Yitro (Jethro). LOGOS follows the MT in each instance without harmonization, consistent with our practice in chapter 2. The LXX reads Iothor. The dual naming likely reflects different narrative traditions preserved faithfully within the text.

2. **"Beyond the wilderness" (v. 1):** Hebrew achar hamidbar, rendered "beyond the wilderness." The preposition achar indicates movement past or behind the wilderness region, suggesting Moses led the flock to a remote area beyond the ordinary grazing range. The LXX reads "into the wilderness" (eis ten eremon), a minor divergence. LOGOS preserves the MT spatial nuance.

3. **"The angel of the LORD" (v. 2) and "the LORD" / "God" (vv. 4, 7):** Hebrew mal'akh YHWH appears in verse 2, introducing the theophany through "the angel of the LORD." Yet by verse 4 the text reads "the LORD saw" (vayyar' YHWH) and "God called" (vayyiqra' Elohim), with no further mention of an intermediary. This seamless transition from messenger to God Himself speaking directly is preserved without smoothing. The divine name YHWH is rendered throughout as "the LORD" (representing the small-caps convention LORD), following the ancient reading tradition of Adonai and consistent with the Masoretic vowel pointing. This rendering is maintained consistently throughout the LOGOS Bible.

4. **"Burning with fire, yet... not consumed" (v. 2):** Hebrew bo'er ba'esh... 'eynenu 'ukkal. The participial construction emphasizes the ongoing, paradoxical state -- the bush is continually burning yet continually not being consumed. The LXX renders this with kaieto puri, ho de batos ou katekaieto ("was burning with fire, but the bush was not being burned up"), preserving the imperfective aspect. LOGOS maintains the durative sense.

5. **"Here I am" (v. 4):** Hebrew hinneni, the patriarchal response of availability and readiness (cf. Genesis 22:1, 1 Samuel 3:4). Rendered "Here I am" rather than the flatter "Yes" or "Present." This is Abraham's word at the Aqedah and Samuel's word at his calling -- its appearance here places Moses in the line of those summoned by God.

6. **"Holy ground" (v. 5):** Hebrew admat qodesh, literally "ground of holiness." Rendered "holy ground" as a natural English construct. This is the first occurrence of the word qodesh ("holy") applied to a physical place in the Torah, marking a foundational theological moment: space itself is sanctified by divine presence.

7. **"The God of your father" (v. 6):** The MT reads singular avikha ("your father"), not the plural "your fathers" that appears in verse 13. LOGOS preserves this distinction. The singular likely refers collectively to the ancestral lineage, or specifically to Amram. The LXX reads "the God of your father" (ho theos tou patros sou), confirming the singular.

8. **"I have surely seen" (v. 7):** Hebrew ra'oh ra'iti, the infinitive absolute construction expressing emphatic certainty. Rendered "I have surely seen" to convey the intensification. This echoes the pattern of emphatic verbal constructions used throughout the Exodus narrative (cf. 2:19 "drew water abundantly"). The LXX uses idon eidon, a corresponding emphatic construction.

9. **"I AM WHO I AM" (v. 14):** Hebrew Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh (אֶהְיֶה אֲשֶׁר אֶהְיֶה). This is the supreme divine self-revelation of the Hebrew Bible. The verb ehyeh is the first-person singular imperfect of hayah ("to be"), encompassing present and future: "I am" and "I will be." The relative asher ("who/that/which") links two identical verbal forms, creating a statement that resists reduction to a single tense or aspect. LOGOS renders it "I AM WHO I AM," capitalizing to mark its status as a divine name-declaration. Alternative renderings include "I WILL BE WHAT I WILL BE" (emphasizing futurity and divine freedom) and "I AM WHAT I AM" (emphasizing self-existence). The LXX reads Ego eimi ho on ("I am the Being" / "I am the One Who Is"), a significant philosophical interpretation that shifts from the dynamic Hebrew verb of becoming to the Greek ontological concept of absolute being. The second statement, "I AM has sent me to you," uses Ehyeh alone as a name -- God's name is a verb, an act, an open presence. LOGOS preserves the traditional rendering while noting that the Hebrew holds all these dimensions simultaneously.

10. **"This is My name forever... My memorial" (v. 15):** Hebrew zeh-shemi le'olam vezeh zikhri ledor dor. The word zikhri ("my memorial/remembrance") is distinct from shem ("name"). God declares both an eternal name and a means of being invoked across generations. The Masoretic text writes le'olam ("forever") with a plene spelling. LOGOS preserves both terms.

11. **"I have surely attended to you" (v. 16):** Hebrew paqod paqadeti, another infinitive absolute. The verb paqad has a wide semantic range: "visit, attend to, take note of, muster." It implies divine intervention following a period of seeming absence. Rendered "I have surely attended to you" to convey both the certainty (emphatic form) and the meaning of caring attention. This phrase echoes Joseph's deathbed prophecy (Genesis 50:24-25): "God will surely attend to you" (paqod yifqod), creating a direct fulfillment link. The LXX uses episkope epeskemmai ("visiting I have visited"), preserving the emphatic form.

12. **"Except by a mighty hand" (v. 19):** Hebrew velo' beyad chazaqah. The phrase is grammatically ambiguous -- it could mean "and not even by a mighty hand" (i.e., Pharaoh will refuse even under compulsion) or "except by a mighty hand" (i.e., only force will compel him). LOGOS follows the latter reading, which aligns with the immediate narrative context of verses 20-22 where God describes the subsequent compulsion. The LXX reads ean me meta cheiros krataias ("unless with a mighty hand"), supporting this interpretation.

13. **"You shall plunder Egypt" (v. 22):** Hebrew venitsaltem et-Mitsrayim. The verb natsal in the Piel means "to strip, plunder, despoil." This is a striking conclusion to the chapter: the enslaved will leave not as destitute refugees but bearing the wealth of their oppressors. The word carries overtones of just recompense -- wages owed for generations of forced labor. The LXX uses skuleusete ("you shall plunder/despoil"), confirming the sense.

### Consistent Term Renderings (Exodus Series)
| Hebrew | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| bney Yisra'el | sons of Israel | Not "children of Israel" or "Israelites" |
| Par'oh | Pharaoh | Retained as title |
| YHWH | the LORD | Small-caps convention; represents the Tetragrammaton |
| Elohim | God | When referring to the God of Israel |
| Ehyeh Asher Ehyeh | I AM WHO I AM | The divine self-revelation, v. 14 |
| Ehyeh | I AM | Abbreviated divine name, v. 14b |
| mal'akh YHWH | the angel of the LORD | The theophanic messenger, v. 2 |
| Yitro | Jethro | Moses' father-in-law; = Reuel of 2:18 |
| Chorev | Horeb | "The mountain of God"; = Sinai |
| admat qodesh | holy ground | First use of qodesh for a physical place |
| paqod paqadeti | I have surely attended to | Echoes Genesis 50:24 |
| natsal (Piel) | plunder | Just recompense for bondage |
| seneh | bush | The burning bush; possible wordplay with Sinai |

— LOGOS
